summary: The Opacity Index scores every category and country spotlight 0-100 on how MEASURABLE the underlying system is (higher = darker). It is distinct from the confidence score, which measures how sure UoF is of its own estimate. A system can be low-confidence yet low-opacity (new but measurable) or high-confidence yet high-opacity (we're sure it's unmeasurable).

categories.name: Consumer Cards
categories.justification: Volumes surface only in quarterly network earnings as aggregates; no public per-transaction data.

categories.name: Digital Wallets
categories.justification: Fragmented private operators; China's PBOC reports only aggregates, Alipay/WeChat internals closed.

categories.name: Bank Transfers
categories.justification: Central banks publish aggregate ACH/RTGS volumes but on a lag and without per-payment detail.

categories.name: ETD (Derivatives)
categories.justification: Exchanges publish daily contract volumes; auditable and near-complete.

categories.name: Equity Markets
categories.justification: Consolidated tape is near real-time and auditable; only dark-pool share is opaque.

categories.name: CEX (Crypto)
categories.justification: Real-time but self-reported and wash-trade inflated; off-exchange internals unverifiable.

categories.name: Bill Payments
categories.justification: Biller aggregators report sparsely; no consolidated public count.

categories.name: E-Commerce
categories.justification: Derived from retail sales aggregates; transaction counts modeled, not observed.

categories.name: ATM Withdrawals
categories.justification: ATM network stats are annual/aggregate; cash-out is the last mile of measurability.

categories.name: IoT & M2M
categories.justification: Machine-to-machine payments are barely instrumented; count is a forward estimate.

categories.name: Payroll
categories.justification: Batch ACH/BACS payroll is buried inside bank-transfer aggregates; no dedicated public count.

categories.name: Government Payments
categories.justification: Published in budget documents annually; auditable but coarse and lagged.

categories.name: BNPL
categories.justification: Private providers disclose GMV, not installment-event counts; multiplier is modeled.

categories.name: Microtransactions
categories.justification: In-game economies are closed; counts inferred from publisher revenue.

categories.name: Stablecoins
categories.justification: On-chain transfers are per-tx and auditable; only issuer reserves add opacity.

categories.name: L1/L2 Blockchain
categories.justification: The measurability anchor: every transaction is public, timestamped, and independently verifiable.

categories.name: Insurance Premiums
categories.justification: Claim/premium counts are modeled from frequency; NAIC compilation is paywalled.

categories.name: Commodities
categories.justification: Exchange-traded volume is measured; physical OTC settlement is largely dark.

categories.name: P2P Transfers
categories.justification: App operators (Venmo, Zelle, Cash App) disclose selectively; no consolidated feed.

categories.name: Forex
categories.justification: The BIS Triennial Survey is authoritative but published every three years — structural latency.

categories.name: Game Sales
categories.justification: Storefront transaction counts inferred from revenue; platforms don't publish counts.

categories.name: Remittances
categories.justification: World Bank publishes annually and formal-only; informal/hawala flows (~60% uplift) are unmeasured.

categories.name: Interbank RTGS
categories.justification: Central banks publish system volumes; auditable but lagged and aggregate.

categories.name: Securities Settlement
categories.justification: CSD/ICSD annual reports give instruction counts; coarse and periodic.

categories.name: Fixed Income
categories.justification: Largely OTC; TRACE covers US corporates only, repo counts are estimated.

categories.name: AI Agents
categories.justification: Nascent; some on-chain agent activity is visible, most is unmeasured.

categories.name: RWA Tokenisation
categories.justification: On-chain and measurable, but small, early, and concentrated in a few issuers.

categories.name: OTC Derivatives
categories.justification: BIS reports notional semiannually; trade counts are inferred, bilateral deals are dark.

spotlights.name: India
spotlights.justification: UPI publishes exact monthly counts with a ~10-day lag; among the most measurable large economies. Residual cash/informal keeps it above the blockchain floor.

spotlights.name: China
spotlights.justification: The largest blind spot in global finance: WMPs, shadow banking, and wallet internals surface only as PBOC aggregates, if at all. The ±6,000 TPS band exceeds whole sectors.

spotlights.name: Brazil
spotlights.justification: Pix is published by the central bank with an open data programme; one of the most measurable payment systems on Earth.
spotlights.source: Banco Central do Brasil Pix statistics

spotlights.name: European Union
spotlights.justification: ECB publishes comprehensive payment statistics and MiCA adds crypto reporting; measurable but lagged and aggregate.

spotlights.name: Japan
spotlights.justification: JPX and bank rails are well-measured, but a large residual cash economy and fragmented QR wallets add coverage gaps.

spotlights.name: United Kingdom
spotlights.justification: Faster Payments and card rails are published, but London's dominant FX/OTC franchise is bilateral and opaque.
